This is looking great - thanks a ton for the many iterations! 

Regarding where to store these, after thinking about it some more it seems like the current options worth discussing are:
1. Add aggregate metrics to all workers.
2. Add aggregate metrics to 0th worker.
3. Change the entire pattern of passing reported metrics to use a more complex dictionary as opposed to the current list.

In the long run I do think we'll move towards (3), but it's out of scope for this PR. There are some additional considerations we'll need to think more about such as the general usability of Callbacks and integration with Tune (currently we only pass worker 0 results and don't support Train Callbacks, but we'll eventually need to support passing aggregate metrics to Tune). 

For (1) vs (2), the tradeoff is verbosity (e.g. if the user wants to log all results to a JSON file) vs. configurability (e.g. if the user wants to log only worker 2 results + aggregate results to TensorBoard). My initial hunch is to go with (2) and see if users request this type of customizability. @jwyyy @Yard1 thoughts?

Also cc @amogkam since this is worth considering for the API redesign.

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that c.session.Accept() blocks until c.session.Close() is closed, which happens in c.Close(), but c.Close() also locks c.m, so we have to release it while c.session.Accept() runs, in order to avoid the deadlock. We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, the deadlocks would still have been happening, but went unnoticed, and that there would have been deadlocked zombie go routines.

[edit: this comment is actually about a different plugin that was mentioned in a related issue]

Though I'd like to include the Leaflet.Control.Layers.Tree plugin in folium, I don't think this PR is the way we should do it, for three reasons:

- The code in this PR contains a specific use case of that JS plugin: radio buttons in separate groups. The JS plugin itself doesn't only contain that, but accepts a generic layer tree.
- It contains custom code in Python as well as the JS template. Ideally, a folium plugin should only wrap the JS plugin. But maybe some is unavoidable here.
- It partially reimplements code of the normal LayerControl class.

IMO an adoption of this JS plugin should have as little code on the folium side as possible and expose the full feature. It should either replace the existing LayerControl class or be compatible with it.

I wonder how that can be done best in folium, since on the Python-side we structure layers a bit different than Leaflet. My best suggestion would be to pass a dictionary with layers to this plugin, then pass that to the JS plugin. And then not use the normal layer control. Something like:

```
layer1 = Layer()
layer2 = FeatureGroup()
layer 3 = Marker()
GroupedLayerControl(
    base_layers=[layer1],
    overlays={
        'Group': [layer2, layer3]
    }
).add_to(m)
````

Note that this mimics the arguments of the JS plugin. That means little code on the folium side. Also note that this doesn't use the `overlay` and `control` parameters of the folium objects, because the tree structure passed to GroupedLayerControl` defines how the layers are used.

I'd be interested to hear how others think about this.

I noticed that LLVM/clang can emit multiple sections with the same name in order to save space in the resulting binary when -ffunction-sections is used. The current code also doesn't handle this case, so using `get_section_by_name()` on such an object file will always return the last section with the non-unique name. See the following example:

```
$ cat two_f.c
void a(){}
void b(){}

$ clang-9 -ffunction-sections -fno-unique-section-names -c -o two_f.o two_f.c

$ readelf -S two_f.o
There are 11 section headers, starting at offset 0x208:

Section Headers:
  [Nr] Name              Type             Address           Offset
       Size              EntSize          Flags  Link  Info  Align
  [ 0]                   NULL             0000000000000000  00000000
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000           0     0     0
  [ 1] .strtab           STRTAB           0000000000000000  000001b0
       0000000000000057  0000000000000000           0     0     1
  [ 2] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     4
  [ 3] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
  [ 4] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000050
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
[...]

$ python3
Python 3.6.9 (default, Jul 17 2020, 12:50:27)
[GCC 8.4.0] on linux
>>> from elftools.elf.elffile import ELFFile
>>> elf = ELFFile(open('two_f.o', 'rb'))
>>> print(elf.get_section_by_name('.text').header)
Container({'sh_name': 1, 'sh_type': 'SHT_PROGBITS', 'sh_flags': 6, 'sh_addr': 0, 'sh_offset': 80, 'sh_size': 6, 'sh_link': 0, 'sh_info': 0, 'sh_addralign': 16, 'sh_entsize': 0})
```

In the same manner, your code will always return the last index (in our example above: 4) for '.text'.

I'm not sure if that's something that needs general fixing but I would like to keep that in mind.

Additionally, it would be nice to have some test files for your code, be it with non-unique section names or not.

OK, it took me a few hours to dig through the whole caster construction (together with @bstaletic), but here I am with another review.

I think one of the things that was confusing to me is that these are 3 independent changes in one PR:

1. Changing the subcaster's `cast_op_type<type>` to `cast_op_type<type&>`

  OK, so you're asking the subcaster how to cast to a `type&` instead of to a `type`. The reason this is the case is that in almost all cases in pybind11, these two types would get the same conversion operator.
  Do you have a good example of when these would be different? This might be good to add in a test (see later remark about tests).
  I think it's OK to change though, since indeed, you actually want a `type&` from the subcaster.

2. Loosening the `static_cast` that does currently not allow for `subcaster::operator const type&()`.

   Fair enough, yes. The repetition of explicitly listing `const` seems a bit ugly, but the `std::remove_const` was already there, so ...
   An alternative would be something like `std::is_convertible<subcaster_cast_op_type, std::reference_wrapper<type>>::value`? But fine to keep as it is now, I think.

3. The `&` in `subcaster.operator subcaster_cast_op_type&()` was completely superfluous already before this PR, I believe (remember we just asserted that it was already an lvalue reference!). So it's a correct change, but independent from this PR.


Now, as I said before: what bothers me a bit is that tests are added, but these tests already didn't fail before the actual changes of this PR. Normally, tests added in a PR would fail before the function changes of the PR?
So should we still add a minimal (!) test with a  `type_caster` that has an `operator const Foo &()`  that's different from `operator Foo &()`? And a `type_caster` that differentiates its `cast_op_type<Bar&>` from `cast_op_type<Bar>`? (do you have a good example of such a case, perhaps; in a smaller version than the full `FreezableInt` file?)

I'd also like to hear from @bstaletic if this summary and assessment makes sense, before officially approving.

Hi @aryamanJgl, thanks for this nice PR!

A few comments/questions from my side:
- I would typically create such finite set constraints as:
```py
z = cp.Variable(len(finite_set), boolean=True)
constraints = [
    cp.sum(cp.multiply(finite_set,z)) == x,
    cp.sum(z) == 1
]
```
This replaces the `n-2` inequality + `1` equality constraints with `2` equality constraints, where `n` is the size of the set. Additionally, (although not currently implemented by CVXPY), some solvers add special support for such [SOS1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special_ordered_set) constraints. Did you consider such an implementation as well?
- You can vectorized the inequality constraints as `z[1:] <= z[:-1]`
- Perhaps the changes to the solving chain should go in `_reductions_for_problem_class()` 
- The tests are failing due to code formatting. Check the [docs](https://www.cvxpy.org/contributing/index.html#code-style) for more info. Don't worry if this does not work, though, as it can also be easily fixed at the end.

Je remets ici une remarque que j'ai déjà mise mais qui est valable pour un grand nombre de fichiers dans cette PR et que je n'ai pas remis à chaque fois : certaines références législatives ajoutées dans les paramètres me semblent non pertinentes. 
Ma compréhension est que dans les barèmes IPP il arrive que plusieurs paramètres soient dans la même feuille. La série de références législatives comprend donc des références pour tous ces paramètres. Le moyen de distinguer à quel paramètre la référence correspond est la date de la référence qui correspond à la date de la modification du paramètre.  
Lors du passage aux paramètres sur openfisca france, chaque paramètre étant mis dans une feuille distincte, il ne me semble donc pas pertinent de garder l'ensemble des références législatives. Il faut garder uniquement les références qui correspondent au paramètre en question et donc qui ont une date qui correspond à une date de modification de la série.

@Bruce-8 instead of indenting all of the existing code, you should use the *guard* pattern, which makes use of an early return:

```python
if not ENABLE_CONTRACT_CHECKING:
    ...
    return
```

The tests are likely failing due to `ENABLE_CONTRACT_CHECKING` being set to `False` in one test and then never being reset back to `True` in other tests. You should look up different ways of handling this type of situation robustly using `pytest`.

Thanks James.  I finally finished going through this.  I thought it was basically fine until I got to the unit tests.  I'm actually pretty concerned about the pixel area of the central pixel changing by as much as it did.  I know we discussed this on some telecons a while back, but looking at the actual numbers involved, I'm pretty sure this is introducing an error that is more that the accuracy Craig had verified the old code.  So probably not such a great thing to change.

I'm proposing an adjustment to the step where you are currently averaging the values from neighboring pixels.  I think instead we should just trust the inner pixel's values and ignore the ones from the outer pixel.  Both sides' values were verified by Craig to be accurate, so either one should actually be fine, but the average may not be.  And I suspect that the corners especially get the wrong value using this procedure.

There are also various style and commentary suggestions I mentioned as I was going.

I tried it out and ran into a few bugs, most likely minor, and added them to the testing suite as `test_more`.

I also made a modification to the repr format:

```
<Array [2021-06-03T10:00, ... 2021-06-03T13:00] type='4 * datetime64'>
```

and

```
<Array [60 minutes, 60 minutes, 60 minutes] type='3 * timedelta64'>
```

The string representation of datetime/timedelta types don't agree with [Datashape](https://datashape.readthedocs.io/en/latest/types.html#date-time-and-datetime), though. Is it possible to fix this? For one thing, the type strings should drop the "64", but is there a way to get timezone information in datetime (e.g. `datetime[tz='UTC']`)?

There is one significant problem, though: whenever we get data from a datetime/timedelta NumpyArray into an `np.ndarray`, it's through an expensive-looking loop in Python. This should be a zero-copy view. The NumpyArray class already has methods like `to_cupy` and `to_jax` that circumvent the buffer interface (and have to be called explicitly by the code layer above it): we need a similar one for `to_numpy` that would be called when the dtype is not something that can be expressed in a buffer.

Actually, we can short-cut this: I created a `view_int64` property on NumpyArray, which just drops the datetime/timedelta metadata so that you can send it through a buffer and add the metadata back in:

```python
>>> akarray
<Array [2021-06-03T10:00, 2021-06-03T11:00] type='2 * datetime64'>
>>> akarray.layout
<NumpyArray format="M8[m]" shape="2" data="0x 78ad9c01 00000000 b4ad9c01 00000000" at="0x562eaa6b1e10"/>
>>> akarray.layout.view_int64
<NumpyArray format="l" shape="2" data="27045240 27045300" at="0x562eaa6b1e10"/>
>>> np.asarray(akarray.layout.view_int64)
array([27045240, 27045300])
>>> np.asarray(akarray.layout.view_int64).view(akarray.layout.format)
array(['2021-06-03T10:00', '2021-06-03T11:00'], dtype='datetime64[m]')
```

Use this to fix the two Python loops over array data.

Hi @rijusougata13, thanks for deciding to contribute. Before we can get this merged, I noticed a few things that can be improved here. This review might seem a bit lengthy, but the suggested changes are not too advanced and I just wanted to explain the motivation between all of these suggestions properly.


Firstly, I think the error messages could be clearer, possibly different for each of the errors.
For example, when an integer with a sign is used and the number is equal to zero, the message could be something like:
> Invalid value, the amount of currency to increase or decrease must be an integer different from zero.

And when the given value is not an integer at all, the message could be something like:
> Invalid value, the argument must be an integer, optionally preceded with a `+` or `-` sign.

This way, it would be clearer to the user what was the issue with the input they given.


Another thing that I've noticed is the lack of `_()` usage, which is required in our project for the strings to get translated if the translation is available. This is easy to miss when contributing for the first time as most projects don't have (or need) the strings to be translatable. This is however very easy to fix and just requires you to wrap user-faced strings in `_()`, e.g. `_("Invalid value!")` instead of `"Invalid value!"`


Lastly, while the approach you chose to handle showing the errors to the user is not wrong, I think it would make more sense to raise `commands.BadArgument` exception in the `__init__` method of `SetParser`. Exceptions are made to handle exceptional cases and invalid input is such a case. This keeps the logic of the command simpler and leaves the handling of the error to global error handlers that Red has.

So to show a full example of all these improvements implemented, here's a piece of code with the existing `RuntimeError` exceptions replaced with `commands.BadArgument` exceptions constructed with meaningful error messages:
```py
        if argument and argument[0] in allowed:
            if self.sum < 0:
                self.operation = "withdraw"
            elif self.sum > 0:
                self.operation = "deposit"
            else:
                raise commands.BadArgument(
                    _(
                        "Invalid value, the amount of currency to increase or decrease"
                        " must be an integer different from zero."
                    )
                )
            self.sum = abs(self.sum)
        elif argument.isdigit():
            self.operation = "set"
        else:
            raise commands.BadArgument(
                _(
                    "Invalid value, the argument must be an integer,"
                    " optionally preceded with a `+` or `-` sign."
                )
            )
```
With this code, there's no need to touch the logic of the command at all and everything will be handled by Red's global error handler.

Hi @Joshix-1,

this seems to be about invocation `ansi2html --partial`.  I'm not a big fan of this pull request as-is because:
- Use of `<style>` is not allowed outside of `<head>` according to https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/style .
- Current users of `--partial` would experience backwards-incompatible behavior
- Working around `--inline` is likely not worth the savings in 99% of environments today, besides purism. I'd be curious how you environment is different.
- (There are no related tests yet.)
- (There are seemingly unrelated changes to white space of the generated code in the PR.)

If this direction is of true importance, maybe adding a new mode argument `--partial-plus-style` would be more clear and backwards-compatible.  My personal vote would be to re-evaluate `--inline` first, though. What do you think?

Best, Sebastian

Thanks for the update @adetsi.  This implementation is better but the failing tests indicate another issue, that `median` can't be calculated for `integer` columns without changing the aggregate column's output type to `float`; trying to calculate the median without this will generate all 0's.  In order to do this, you'd need to set `t_tscalar median_average` explicitly, as well as setting the associated metadata for the schema transition in a few other places around the codebase. (this is done already for e.g. `count` which goes from any type to `integer`, if you need an example to grep for).

In lieu of this, it would be easier to just implement this behavior for `float` columns instead of _numeric_ ones, by changing the feature-check from `is_numeric` to `is_floating_point`.  `integer` columns will then stay `integer` when applied `median()` and exhibit the old behavior, while columns which are float will use the new behavior. This would be an acceptable alternative if the former implementation for `integer`-column-type-upgrading is too complex (it may involve updating python and the UI as well).

See inline review notes below, there are also a few small style and efficiency improvements, and we should preserve the current test inputs and their integer columns.

I went through constants.py. My general guidelines
* I'm fine breaking compatibility here, but lets be consistent moving forward.
* I prefer spelling out names, eg ONBOARD not ONBRD
* I prefer not repeating the name of the enum in the value. So ActiveLevel.ABOVE is better than ActiveLevel.ABOVE_LEVEL
* I don't think we can lead with numbers, eg 1_VALUE, so we try to spell out numbers. I think for consistency we should do that everywhere.

Before we hack on the generator / metadata, lets make sure @bkeryan and I agree with our guidelines.

EDIT: Oh, and these breaking changes are going to break many existing tests. We can't change them in this PR since we're building `nidaqmx` out of source still, but we will need to update them at some point. Let's make sure we have that tracked in the Features/User Stories or something. I'll at least notice when we switch the `pyproject.toml` to be from generated. I'll want to ensure all tests pass then.

Thank you, @sairina . The overall direction is looking great. I have two high-level suggestions for now that I think would be good to address.

For later reference, the  `NotificationsRoot` is visually responsible for the following four components which I'll call the messaging components

| `AuthMessage` | `AppError` | `GlobalSnackbar` | `UpdateNotification` |
| -------------------- | -------------- | ----------------------- | --------------------------- |
| ![AuthMessage](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009457-d918fbe9-0ceb-40b8-8281-4d495788c9f1.png) | ![AppError](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009448-ca417992-744e-4af2-97e9-5aa9c9aa62d3.png)  | ![GlobalSnackbar](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009461-6488be67-7021-485d-9197-443deda639eb.png) | ![UpdateNotification](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009462-9a5f0118-dc6f-4e3f-8f61-925b75d1c307.png) |

### (1) Decoupling of unnecessary style and layout information (including deletion of related props)

 I’d suggest removing all styles that are not directly related to the messaging components. That’s the majority of computed styles (e.g. `mainWrapperStyles` , `contentStyles`) as well as styles defined in `<style>` block. It’d be fine to keep styles that are unnecessary to position these components on a page if there are some. This also applies to related props: `fullScreen` , `hasSidebar` ,`maxMainWidth` , and `marginBottom`.

**Reason**

`NotificationsRoot` is planned to be used in the following way from plugin index pages:

```javascript
// LearnIndex

<template>
  <NotificationsRoot>
    <router-view />
  </NotificationsRoot>
</template>
```

One of the points of `CoreBase` refactor is to allow each page of the plugin that’s rendered in the `<router-view />` to have full control over its layout, including margins, padding, etc. to gain more flexibility. That wouldn’t be fully possible if we kept unnecessary layout and style dependencies in the `NotificationsRoot`.

### (2) Decoupling loading state from the default slot

I’d suggest removing  `v-if="!loading"` from [the whole wrapper div here](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/9128/files#diff-4a205996a86d148f2aeb0e7a84a2f6ea6325fe25f0f548eae3230a4390abdce4R9) and only using it directly on components that need it, that’s `AuthMessage` and `AppError` . For example

```javascript
<KPageContainer v-if="!loading && notAuthorized">
  <AuthMessage
    :authorizedRole="authorizedRole"
    :header="authorizationErrorHeader"
    :details="authorizationErrorDetails"
  />
</KPageContainer>
```

**Reason**

Keeping `v-if="!loading"` on the div that’s also wrapping [the default slot](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/9128/files#diff-4a205996a86d148f2aeb0e7a84a2f6ea6325fe25f0f548eae3230a4390abdce4R33) has unhappy consequences. Consider the situation demonstrated in the following example when we’d try to set loading state from one of the pages of the Learn plugin:

```javascript
// LearnIndex

<template>
  <NotificationsRoot>
    <router-view />
  </NotificationsRoot>
</template>
```

```javascript
// Library (rendered within the <router-view /> of LearnIndex)

<script>

export default {
  created() {
    state.loading = true
    fetchData().then(() => state.loading = false)
  }
}

</script>
```

At the moment we set `state.loading = true` in the `created` hook, the Library component would get destroyed (therefore `state.loading = false` would never get called) if we kept `v-if="!loading"` as is in the `NotificationsRoot` . This would make all logic around the loading state rigid and difficult to refactor later (and debug - true story :) - for example, we wouldn't be able to use the global loading state at all in a way shown in the example above (and we actually already need to do this in some cases and use hacks to overcome the current limitation).

---

To sum it up, this means we’d only keep things that are absolutely unnecessary for notifications and messaging components in the `NotificationsRoot`  (and if we find out we need something more from it in the future, we can do that when a need arises while considering architecture and alternative options to solving that particular need).

> So, am I correct in thinking that this is based off [this work by gardner](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Jacob-Gardner-2/publication/271195338_Bayesian_Optimization_with_Inequality_Constraints/links/54bfd8ca0cf28a63249ff25c/Bayesian-Optimization-with-Inequality-Constraints.pdf)?

Yes! This was the approach discussed in #326.

> In that case, this handles situations where checking feasibility is just as expensive as assessing the objective function? It appears that the objective function is still assessed for unfeasible cases, but that the likelihood of probing these regions should decrease as the GP models get better?

Yes, or at least very expensive.

> What about the more common scenario where in/feasibility is known in advance? it seems like in that case, this is quite a complex and expensive approach to deal with constraints?

I think you are right. The reason why I went for this approach was 1) the robustness/versatility and 2) because the paper seemed interesting. Nonetheless, it is probably unnecessarily complex for many usecases.

> Do you think there's an elegant way to handle both scenarios - the case where the feasible regions are known in advance and the case where they are only known retrospectively?

It's probably feasible to add some kind of `mode="simple"` or `mode="gardner"` to the `ConstraintModel`. How would you propose handling easy constraints in practice?
